abstract: This paper introduces a visual-first methodological approach for analysing multimodal climate denial and conspiracy theory discourse on Instagram. The authors argue that existing text-centric methods are insufficient for studying platforms where images carry primary communicative weight, and propose a framework that foregrounds visual elements while integrating accompanying textual and contextual cues. The methodology is positioned as a contribution to discourse and media studies concerned with how conspiracy narratives circulate on image-driven social media.
key_claims: Climate denial discourse on Instagram is fundamentally multimodal and cannot be adequately understood through text-focused analytical methods alone.
A visual-first methodology offers a more faithful approach to analysing conspiracy theory narratives on image-centric platforms.
Integrating visual analysis with textual and contextual interpretation reveals narrative strategies of climate denial that would otherwise be missed.
methods: Development and articulation of a novel visual-first methodological framework for multimodal discourse analysis.
Application of the methodology to Instagram content related to climate denial and conspiracy theories.
Combination of visual analysis with attention to captions, hashtags, and platform context.
findings: Instagram climate denial discourse relies heavily on visual rhetorical strategies that text-first methods underrepresent.
Multimodal narrative structures play a central role in how conspiracy theories about climate are constructed and circulated on the platform.
contributions: Introduces a novel visual-first methodology for studying multimodal discourse on image-driven social media.
Extends discourse analytic tools for examining climate denial and conspiracy theory communication.
Provides a replicable analytical framework applicable to other domains of visual conspiracy discourse on platforms like Instagram.
framing: The paper is situated within multimodal discourse analysis, media and communication studies, and emerging scholarship on conspiracy theories and climate denial on social media. It responds to a perceived gap in methodologies that adequately address image-first platforms, positioning visual analysis as central rather than supplementary.
